learn.title: EV Charging Levels Compared
learn.intro: Charging speed determines how quickly electric‑vehicle owners can get back on the road, shaping adoption rates and grid demand. Understanding Level 1, Level 2, and DC fast charging helps planners build a resilient, user‑friendly charging network for the clean‑mobility transition.
learn.c1_text: Level 1 uses a standard 120 V outlet, adding about 3–5 mi of range per hour. It’s like topping up a coffee mug; you can leave the car plugged overnight and wake to a full charge, perfect for low‑mileage drivers.
learn.c2_text: Level 2 delivers 240 V, providing 15–30 mi of range per hour. Comparable to a kitchen blender: it spins faster, cutting charge time to 4–6 hours for an 80 kWh battery, common in workplaces and fast‑lane parking.
learn.c3_text: DC fast chargers supply 400–900 V, adding 60–80 mi in 10‑20 minutes. Think of a sprint: the battery jumps from 10 % to 80 % in a coffee break, essential for long‑distance travel and corridor planning.
learn.stat3: DC fast chargers can deliver up to 350 kW power
learn.stat4: Average EV driver needs 4 hours of Level 2 charge per week

learn.title: Rooftop Solar & Net Metering in India
learn.intro: India’s soaring electricity demand and abundant sunshine make rooftop solar a key decarbonisation tool. Net metering lets owners sell excess power back to the grid, turning homes into mini‑power plants and accelerating the nation’s clean‑energy goals.
learn.c1_text: Photovoltaic cells convert sunlight into DC electricity; an inverter changes it to AC for household use. A 5 kW rooftop system can generate ~6,500 kWh/year—enough to power four average Indian families.
learn.c2_text: When a rooftop system produces more than the house consumes, the surplus flows to the grid and the owner earns a credit at the same tariff. Credits roll over monthly, reducing the next bill’s amount.
learn.c3_text: India’s Ministry of New & Renewable Energy set a 40 GW rooftop target for 2030. States follow a uniform net‑metering rule: 1 kWh exported = 1 kWh imported, encouraging investment especially in Delhi and Maharashtra.
learn.stat3: Net metering covers ~30 % of rooftop systems

learn.title: The Duck Curve Challenge
learn.intro: High solar output midday drives down net load, creating a “duck‑shaped” demand curve that plummets and then spikes at sunset. This rapid ramp stresses conventional generators and calls for smarter grid operations to keep lights on.
learn.c1_title: 🦆 What the Duck Curve Looks Like
learn.c1_text: Midday solar can cut net demand by 30‑40 % in California, forming the low‑point of the duck’s belly. After sunset, demand rebounds sharply—up to 10 % of total load in 30 minutes—requiring fast‑ramping resources.
learn.c2_text: To smooth the ramp, operators add battery storage, demand‑response, and fast‑gas turbines. A 1 GW battery can shave 2‑3 GW of ramp, acting like a shock absorber for the grid.
learn.c3_text: India’s solar surge (≈30 % of daytime load in Gujarat) creates similar ramps. The Ministry is piloting 2 GW of pumped hydro and 1 GW of battery storage to mitigate evening peaks.
learn.stat1: US: 2023 peak ramp ≈ 7 GW in CA
learn.stat2: Battery storage installed in US: 12 GW (2024)
learn.stat4: Pumped hydro capacity in India: 5 GW

learn.title: Perovskite Solar Cells Breakthrough
learn.intro: Perovskite solar cells promise higher efficiencies and lower production costs than traditional silicon, potentially reshaping the photovoltaic market and speeding up global decarbonisation.
learn.c1_text: Perovskites are crystal structures (ABX₃) that absorb light efficiently. Lab cells have reached 26 % efficiency—close to the Shockley‑Queisser limit—using thin‑film layers only a few hundred nanometers thick.
learn.c2_text: They can be printed on flexible substrates, similar to ink‑jet printing, cutting material use by 70 % compared with wafer‑based silicon. This opens low‑cost, large‑area deployment on roofs and façades.
learn.c3_text: India’s Ministry of Science & Technology funds a ₹1,200 crore perovskite program. Pilot projects aim to install 100 MW of perovskite‑silicon tandem modules by 2027, boosting rooftop output by 15 %.
learn.stat4: Global perovskite market forecast: $1.2 B by 2030

learn.title: Carbon Markets Power Clean Energy
learn.intro: Carbon markets assign a price to greenhouse‑gas emissions, creating financial incentives for renewable projects. By buying credits, companies fund clean‑energy builds, turning climate goals into market‑driven investments.
learn.c1_text: One credit equals one tonne of CO₂ avoided. Companies that emit less than their allowance sell excess credits, while high‑emitters buy them, similar to a marketplace for “clean‑air vouchers.”
learn.c2_text: Cap‑and‑trade sets an overall emissions ceiling that declines annually. The price signal—often $50‑$100 per tonne—makes renewable projects like wind farms financially attractive compared to fossil plants.
learn.c3_text: India’s Perform, Achieve, and Trade (PAT) scheme has generated 30 MtCO₂e credits (2022). Renewable generators earn Additionality Credits, which can be sold to industrial users to meet their reduction targets.
learn.stat3: IEA: $1.2 trillion private clean‑energy finance in 2023
learn.stat4: India: 2 GW renewable projects financed via credits (2023)

learn.title: Concentrated Solar Power Explained
learn.intro: Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) uses mirrors to focus sunlight onto a receiver, generating high‑temperature heat that drives a turbine. Its built‑in storage makes it a reliable, dispatchable renewable source.
learn.c1_text: Parabolic troughs or heliostat fields concentrate sunlight up to 1,000 × onto a fluid—often molten salt—heating it to 560 °C. One megawatt of CSP can produce the same electricity as a coal plant without emissions.
learn.c2_text: Molten‑salt tanks store heat for 6‑15 hours, allowing CSP to generate power after sunset. It’s like a solar‑powered battery, delivering steady output during peak evening demand.
learn.c3_text: India’s 500 MW CSP plant in Rajasthan (operational 2025) will supply power to 1 million homes and provide 8 hours of storage, supporting grid stability in the desert region.
learn.stat4: India aims for 2 GW CSP by 2030
